name: roc
whatItMeasures: The Rate of Change measures the percentage change in price between the current price and the price a set number of periods ago. It is a pure momentum indicator that oscillates around zero — positive values mean price has increased over the lookback period, negative values mean it has decreased. ROC quantifies the speed at which price is changing.

parameters.name: Period
parameters.rationale: A 9-period lookback captures short-to-medium-term momentum and is effective for identifying accelerating or decelerating trends without excessive noise.

howToRead: ROC above zero means price is higher than it was 9 periods ago (positive momentum).
ROC below zero means price is lower than it was 9 periods ago (negative momentum).
ROC crossing from negative to positive can signal the start of an uptrend.
ROC crossing from positive to negative can signal the start of a downtrend.
Extreme ROC values (very high or very low) can indicate unsustainable momentum that may reverse.

signalRules.buy: ROC crosses above zero (momentum turning positive) or rebounds from an extreme negative reading.
signalRules.sell: ROC crosses below zero (momentum turning negative) or reverses from an extreme positive reading.
signalRules.neutral: ROC near zero with no clear directional momentum.

limitations: ROC is unbounded, making it difficult to define consistent overbought/oversold levels across different assets.
As a single-lookback indicator, ROC can be noisy and prone to whipsaws around the zero line.
ROC does not account for volume or trend strength — it only measures price change.
The indicator is highly sensitive to the lookback period choice; different periods can yield conflicting signals.
ROC works best as a confirmation or filter tool rather than a standalone signal generator.
evidence: Backtesting across 30 DJIA stocks showed ROC achieving a 66% win rate. While this is a moderate win rate compared to other oscillators, ROC's primary value is as a false signal filter — confirming or denying signals generated by other indicators. When momentum (ROC) disagrees with a buy or sell signal from another indicator, the signal is more likely to be false. This filtering role makes ROC a valuable component of multi-indicator systems.
